featured_videos.editorial_title: This Is The New Butter (And You’ll Be FORCED To Eat It!)
featured_videos.commentary: Okay, so the butter conversation is getting real, and people are *mad* about it. What's hitting different here is that this isn't just "big food adds weird stuff" anymore, it's the realization that ingredient standards are shifting without anyone really noticing. The comments are pulling up the stuff that haunts people, right. remembering when Breyers actually tasted like ice cream, or how one ingredient list says "real lemon" while another says "artificial" for basically the same product. It's the betrayal feeling that gets to folks more than the actual chemistry. And yeah, local farms are suddenly looking way smarter than they did five years ago.

featured_videos.editorial_title: This New Carnivore Study Changes EVERYTHING
featured_videos.commentary: The comments on this one tell the real story. You've got people reversing autoimmune conditions, getting off multiple medications, losing serious weight, and then there's the cardiologist who started questioning his entire medical school training based on what he's seeing in his patients' bloodwork. That's not anecdotal noise, that's practitioners noticing a pattern they can't ignore. What strikes me most is the consistency, the specificity of what's changing. Not vague "I feel better" claims, but Graves disease gone, MS injections stopped, 90 pounds down. The research backing this stuff up matters, but honestly, when you see that many people reporting the same metabolic turnarounds independently, the mechanism research almost becomes secondary. The real question isn't whether carnivore works anymore, it's why mainstream medicine is still so slow to catch up.

featured_videos.editorial_title: When the Doctor Becomes the Patient: CARNIVORE (with Kelly Hogan and Dr. Jocelyn Foran)
featured_videos.commentary: Real talk: when doctors have to eat their own advice, everything changes. Dr. Foran's story hits because she didn't stumble into carnivore as some wellness trend, she found it out of desperation when ulcerative colitis was destroying her quality of life. The community comments are showing the same pattern we're seeing everywhere right now, people reversing conditions that medicine told them were permanent, things like angina, MS symptoms, and fatty liver disease. That MS comment especially, someone cycling again after thinking mobility was gone, that's the kind of story that makes people actually believe this works because it's not theory, it's lived experience.

featured_videos.editorial_title: What Research Says About Breaking Weight-Loss Plateaus (With 16-year Carnivore, Kelly Hogan)
featured_videos.commentary: Kelly hits on something I see constantly in coaching: people are doing everything "right" and still stuck, so they assume they're broken. They're not. The dairy conversation alone. someone losing 15 pounds just by cutting it out after two years of stalling. shows how individual these plateaus really are. What matters most here is the permission Kelly's giving people to stop white-knuckling the food and actually enjoy the process again. That shift from "I'm failing" to "maybe I just need to recalibrate my approach" is where the real breakthrough happens, and it's way less about the mechanics of TDEE and way more about trusting yourself again.

Shawn nails it here, and the comments section is having fun with it for good reason. A million dollars to chase immortality while looking worse than someone who actually prioritizes sleep, sunlight, and real movement is peak longevity theater. The irony is thick, the audience sees it, and the gap between the promise and the reality is impossible to ignore. Real health isn't a subscription service or a billionaire's vanity project, it's fundamentals done consistently, carnivore or otherwise.

featured_videos.editorial_title: This Carnivore Pizza Replaced My Junk Food Cravings
featured_videos.commentary: One thing I've noticed coaching people is that pizza cravings don't actually disappear on carnivore, they just evolve. The comments here show exactly what matters most: someone reversed heart disease, lost 60 pounds, and got off sleep apnea meds. That's not happening because pizza tastes good. It's happening because when you remove the foods that were causing inflammation and blood sugar chaos, your body finally gets to heal. The pizza recipe is just the permission slip that makes the transition feel less like deprivation. That cottage cheese and pork rind base is genius because it hits the texture and satisfaction without the vegetable oils and seed oils that were keeping people stuck in the cravings cycle in the first place.
